prompt: Every quarter we add headcount to the on-call rotation but the number of escalations keeps climbing right back up within a month or two. We've done this three times now and nothing actually gets better. What's going on?
note: Recurring problem where the same fix keeps failing — classic systems-archetype pattern.

prompt: A test that's been green for months just failed once on CI. How worried should I actually be that there's a real regression versus it just being flaky, given it's failed maybe twice all year?
note: Updating a probability estimate given a base rate and one new observation.

prompt: I've spent two days tuning the cache eviction parameters and I keep finding tiny improvements. I have ten other things to ship this sprint. How do I know when this is good enough to walk away from?
note: Setting a stopping criterion for a decision under time pressure — satisficing.

prompt: Leadership wants me to lead the design of the on-chain settlement layer for our new payments product. I've never worked on anything blockchain-related. Should I take this on or push back?
note: Evaluating an opportunity that sits outside one's domain expertise.

prompt: My team can't agree on how to approach a new initiative. Some people want a detailed upfront plan, others want to just experiment and see what sticks. How do I figure out which approach actually fits this kind of problem?
note: Matching methodology to the nature of the problem — clear vs complex vs chaotic.

prompt: We're about to sign a two-year contract with the same database vendor we've always used. The stakes are high and I want to make sure we're choosing them for the right reasons and not just out of habit before I commit. Walk me through how to sanity-check this.
note: High-stakes decision where the goal is to counteract anchoring/status-quo distortions.

prompt: I have a strong gut feeling this architecture is wrong, but I can't articulate why and the proposal looks fine on paper. The decision is expensive to reverse. Should I trust the instinct or override it with the analysis?
note: Deciding when to trust intuition vs deliberate analysis on a high-stakes call.

prompt: I'm starting a side business but the market is brand new and I genuinely can't predict demand or who the customers even are. Every business plan I write feels like fiction. How should I actually get moving with what I've got?
note: Novel, unpredictable domain where you start from available means rather than goals.

prompt: Our referral signups suddenly started doubling every week with no extra marketing spend, and now it's plateauing just as fast. I want to understand the underlying dynamics driving both the spike and the flattening.
note: Reinforcing growth then a balancing limit — feedback loop dynamics.

prompt: Before I provision infrastructure, roughly how many database write operations per second should I expect if we onboard a city of about a million people and each active user does a handful of actions a day? I have no real traffic data yet.
note: Order-of-magnitude estimate by decomposing into estimable factors with no data.

prompt: Everyone tells me I have to use an off-the-shelf auth provider, but our requirements are weird and none of them fit cleanly. I want to ignore what everyone does and reason about what authentication actually fundamentally requires for our case.
note: Questioning assumptions and rebuilding from fundamentals when convention doesn't fit.

prompt: We had a two-hour outage yesterday because a deploy went out with a bad config. I need to write the post-incident report and I want to get to the real reason this happened, not just blame the engineer who hit the button.
note: Incident root-cause analysis with bias guards against blaming the proximate cause.

prompt: We're planning the public launch of our new mobile app next month. I want to put together a solid plan, and I'm particularly worried about all the ways this could blow up in our faces on launch day.
note: Planning by enumerating failure paths and systematically avoiding them.

prompt: Our note-taking app has tons of features but engagement is flat. People sign up and drift away. I don't think we actually understand what users are really trying to accomplish when they reach for our product. How do I dig into that?
note: Understanding the underlying progress/job users are hiring the product for.

prompt: We're choosing between four message-queue technologies for a critical pipeline. There are a dozen competing factors — throughput, ops burden, cost, team familiarity, vendor risk. I need a rigorous way to weigh them all and defend the choice to the board.
note: Structured multi-criteria decision analysis for a high-stakes engineering choice.

prompt: Our whole onboarding system feels broken in a hundred small ways and I have limited engineering time. Instead of fixing everything, where's the one or two places I could intervene that would actually shift the whole system the most?
note: Finding where a small change yields the largest systemic effect.

prompt: I'm picking a frontend framework for a project we'll have to maintain for the next decade. One option is the new hotness everyone's tweeting about; the other has been around forever and is kind of boring. How should I think about which will still be around in ten years?
note: Predicting tool longevity based on how long it has already survived.

prompt: Our dashboards all say the service is healthy and latency is fine, but customers keep emailing that the app feels slow and broken. Our metrics and what users experience don't line up at all. Where do I even start?
note: The model/dashboard diverges from the real territory users experience.

prompt: My boss wants me to commit to a delivery date for a feature with a lot of unknowns and integration work I can't fully scope yet. My honest estimate is six weeks. What date should I actually put on the calendar?
note: Building a buffer for unknown unknowns into a commitment.

prompt: I'm facing a genuinely tangled decision about whether to rebuild our platform: there's technical risk, team morale, competitive timing, and budget all wrapped together. One way of looking at it keeps leaving me with blind spots. How can I analyze something this multifaceted thoroughly?
note: Complex high-stakes problem that needs multiple lenses to avoid blind spots.

prompt: I've got a gnarly engineering decision in front of me and I honestly don't know what the best way to even reason about it is. Can you help me figure out the right way to approach thinking about this?
note: User explicitly needs help choosing how to reason — the router's entry point.

prompt: I keep reaching for the same problem-solving playbook out of habit, and it's not working on this new class of problem. How do I match my approach to the kind of problem I'm actually dealing with this time?
note: Matching a reasoning tool to the problem when the usual approach fails.

prompt: A handful of users are seeing stale data. One theory is a complicated race condition across three services plus a CDN edge bug; another is just that we forgot to bust a cache key. Which should I chase first?
note: Preferring the explanation with the fewest assumptions when debugging.

prompt: We're in the middle of an active outage right now, the situation keeps shifting every few minutes as we get new alerts, and I'm leading the response. How do I stay on top of this and keep making good calls fast?
note: Rapid, adaptive decision cycle for a fast-changing live situation.

prompt: My team could spend the next quarter building our own analytics pipeline, or we could pay for a SaaS tool and spend that quarter on our core product instead. The build is cheaper on paper. How should I think about which is really the better use of us?
note: Evaluating a choice by what's given up, not just direct cost — build vs buy.

prompt: We're kicking off a six-month replatforming project tomorrow and everyone's really optimistic. Before we lock in the plan, I'd like the team to soberly surface the risks we might be glossing over in all the excitement.
note: At kickoff, surfacing failure causes before optimism hides them.

prompt: My PM keeps asking me for a single date when the migration will be done, and I keep giving one and being wrong. I want to communicate my uncertainty honestly and get better at predicting these over time. How should I frame my estimates?
note: Expressing confidence as ranges and improving calibration over time.

prompt: We're two weeks from launching a public API that handles user payments. Before it goes live I want someone to think like an attacker and try to poke holes in our design and find the ways it could be abused.
note: Deliberately attacking your own system to find weaknesses pre-launch.

prompt: I have a stable, well-paid senior role, but I've been offered a much riskier position at an early startup doing work I'm passionate about. I'm torn and afraid of making the wrong call. How do I think about this from the perspective of who I want to be?
note: Career decision framed around future-self regret.

prompt: We're debating whether to switch our primary database from Postgres to a managed NoSQL store. Some folks want to just decide in the standup; others want a month-long evaluation. How much process does a decision like this actually warrant?
note: Matching decision rigor to how hard the choice is to reverse.

prompt: Our API p99 latency tripled last Tuesday and I have no idea why. There are several plausible causes. I want to investigate this in a disciplined way instead of randomly changing things and hoping.
note: Hypothesis-test-revise approach to a performance investigation.

prompt: We're thinking about adding aggressive rate limiting to stop a few abusive accounts. It seems like a clean fix, but I want to think through how this might ripple out and affect our legitimate power users and integrations down the line.
note: Reasoning about downstream and unintended consequences of a policy change.

prompt: A stakeholder asked me to build a reporting feature but the request is super vague and I suspect they haven't fully thought through what they actually need. How do I draw out the real requirements without just building the wrong thing?
note: Using systematic questioning to surface hidden requirements and assumptions.

prompt: I'm convinced we should go with the microservices approach and a colleague is pushing monolith. Before I write my recommendation I want to make sure I'm genuinely engaging with the best version of their argument and not a strawman.
note: Engaging the strongest form of the opposing position in a design review.

prompt: Our distributed job system occasionally grinds to a halt even though no single service is overloaded and every component looks healthy on its own. The weird behavior only shows up when everything's running together. Help me make sense of it.
note: Emergent, non-linear behavior arising from component interactions.

prompt: Our CI pipeline takes 40 minutes. I've been optimizing the test parallelization and the Docker layer caching for weeks but the total time barely moves. I feel like I'm polishing the wrong things. Where should I actually look?
note: Throughput won't improve until the actual bottleneck is found and addressed.

prompt: We can't actually load-test against ten million concurrent users, and the architecture review is tomorrow. I want to reason through what would happen to this design if usage suddenly jumped a hundredfold, purely as a hypothetical.
note: Testing a design through a hypothetical scenario when empirical testing is impractical.

prompt: Product wants deploys to be instant for fast iteration, but compliance wants every change to go through a slow multi-stage approval. These two requirements seem fundamentally at odds and I'm stuck trying to satisfy both. Is there a clever way out?
note: Resolving a technical contradiction where two requirements seem mutually exclusive.

prompt: Our settings page has grown to 60 toggles over the years and it's overwhelming for everyone. My instinct is to keep adding 'smart defaults' on top, but I wonder if the answer is the opposite. How should I approach cleaning this up?
note: Improvement through removal and subtraction rather than addition.

prompt: Can you rename the variable `usrCnt` to `userCount` everywhere it appears in this repo?
note: Mechanical refactor — no mental model needed.

prompt: What's the bash syntax for a for loop that iterates over the files in a directory?
note: Routine syntax lookup.

prompt: There's a typo in the README — 'recieve' should be 'receive'. Can you fix it?
note: Trivial text fix.

prompt: Add a console.log right before the return statement in the `handleSubmit` function so I can see the payload.
note: Trivial code edit.

prompt: Can you pretty-print and format this JSON blob for me with two-space indentation?
note: Mechanical formatting task.

prompt: What does `git stash` actually do, and how do I get my changes back afterward?
note: Factual tool-usage question.

prompt: Convert this callback-based function to use async/await instead.
note: Routine code transformation.

prompt: Write me a regex that matches a basic email address.
note: Routine pattern-writing task.

prompt: How do I vertically and horizontally center a div using flexbox?
note: Routine CSS how-to.

prompt: What's the npm command to install lodash as a dev dependency?
note: Trivial command lookup.

prompt: Write a SQL query that joins the orders table to the customers table on customer_id and returns the customer name and order total.
note: Straightforward query writing.

prompt: How do I sort an array of objects by their `createdAt` field in descending order in JavaScript?
note: Routine coding how-to.

prompt: Add a port mapping so the container exposes 8080 to the host in this docker-compose file.
note: Mechanical config edit.

prompt: How do I read an environment variable in a Node.js script?
note: Routine API question.

prompt: I just committed to the wrong branch. How do I undo the last commit but keep my changes staged?
note: Routine git how-to.

prompt: Bump the version of the express dependency to the latest 4.x in package.json.
note: Mechanical dependency edit.

prompt: We had a payment-processing failure that affected about 3% of checkouts for an hour last night. I need to figure out the underlying cause and write it up so it doesn't happen again.
note: Post-incident root cause — fits 5-whys, scientific method, KT, or systems.

prompt: We need to choose a database for a new service that we'll be stuck with for a long time, and I want to make a smart, well-reasoned choice we won't regret.
note: Long-lived irreversible tech choice — reversibility, KT, Lindy, or opportunity cost all defensible.

prompt: We have engineering capacity for exactly one big feature next quarter and three teams are lobbying hard for theirs. How do I decide which one actually deserves the slot?
note: Prioritization under scarcity — opportunity cost, JTBD, or KT all apply.

prompt: We shipped a feature we were sure people wanted and almost nobody is using it. I want to understand why and decide what to do next.
note: Diagnosing failed adoption — could be JTBD, hypothesis-testing, or root-cause.

prompt: We're about to 10x our user base after a marketing push and I'm nervous about what's going to break. Help me get ahead of it.
note: Anticipating failure under growth — pre-mortem, inversion, capacity estimate, or buffer.

prompt: There are five different theories floating around the team about why the export feature corrupts large files. I need to cut through the noise and figure out which is right.
note: Competing debugging hypotheses — scientific method, Occam, or 5-whys.

prompt: Do we pause feature work for a month to pay down our worst tech debt, or keep shipping and live with it? I keep going back and forth.
note: Tech-debt tradeoff — opportunity cost, second-order effects, or constraint.

prompt: Engineering and product have been at each other's throats over the same roadmap disagreement for a year. Every truce falls apart within weeks. How do I actually break this cycle?
note: Recurring organizational conflict — archetypes, systems, or feedback loops.

prompt: How confident should I be that we'll hit the Q3 launch date given everything we know right now, and how should I keep updating that as we go?
note: Calibrated confidence that updates over time — probabilistic or Bayesian.

prompt: Our service mesh has gotten incredibly complicated and most of the moving parts may not be earning their keep. I want to make the whole thing simpler and easier to reason about.
note: Simplification — removal (via negativa), parsimony, or systemic leverage.

prompt: Our sales funnel converts terribly and I have one engineer for two weeks. I want to find the single change that would move the numbers the most.
note: Finding the highest-impact single intervention — constraint or leverage point.

prompt: Our current product is doing okay but plateauing, and there's a chance to pivot to something with much bigger upside but real downside risk. I don't want to look back in five years and wish we'd been braver — or that we'd been smarter.
note: Strategic pivot weighing future regret, irreversibility, and uncertainty.

prompt: Daily active users dropped 15% over the weekend and nobody changed anything we know of. Marketing blames the product, product blames an algorithm tweak, and I need to get to the bottom of it.
note: Surprising metric drop with competing explanations — investigation or root-cause.

prompt: My team is really confident about this Q4 plan and honestly so am I, which is exactly why it's making me a little uneasy. Can you help me pressure-test our thinking before we commit?
note: Guarding against overconfidence in a plan — debiasing, pre-mortem, or red team.

prompt: A friend wants me to invest a chunk of my savings in their biotech startup. I understand software but I know nothing about drug development or that market. How should I think about whether to do this?
note: Decision outside one's domain — competence boundary, regret, or downside buffer.

prompt: My team is split between two API designs and the debate has gone in circles for days. I want a fair way to settle which one is actually better.
note: Settling a stalled design debate fairly — steel-man, structured criteria, or scenario test.
